video_title: US Strikes Iran; Stock Rout; SpaceX IPO Oversubscribed | Horizons Middle East & Africa 6/11/2026
gist: A Bloomberg 'Horizons Middle East & Africa' markets broadcast (6/11/2026) anchored by a second day of US strikes on Iran amid a fragile 'no war, no peace' ceasefire, a 3-year-high US headline CPI print that left core benign, a massively oversubscribed $75B SpaceX IPO, and a widely-expected ECB hike into stagflation. Despite the title's 'stock rout,' equities are described as resilient YTD with US futures turning higher.

themes.theme: Iran Conflict as the Oil/Growth Swing Factor

insights.claim: The current US-Iran escalation is framed as a spike within an ongoing 'messy ceasefire' rather than a planned long-term escalation, with strikes focused on military targets (surveillance, communications, air defense) signaling both sides seek a diplomatic offramp.
insights.speaker: William Roebuck, EVP Arab Gulf States Institute, former US Ambassador to Bahrain
insights.so_what: Base case is reversion to 'no war, no peace,' capping oil's geopolitical premium unless an unpredictable event breaks the guardrails.
insights.quote: I EXPECT IT TO REVERT BACK MAY BE TO THE MESSY CEASE-FIRE THAT WE HAVE BEEN SEEING, NO WAR, NO PEACE

insights.claim: Iran has calibrated its retaliation to attack regional countries rather than escalate uncontrollably, demonstrating strategic coherence and self-imposed guardrails despite months of military pressure.
insights.speaker: William Roebuck
insights.so_what: The presence of Iranian guardrails is the key variable suppressing tail-risk pricing in oil; watch for signs they break down.

insights.claim: Trump faces a domestic political bind where there is very little support for cutting a deal with Iran or making the concessions likely required, making a near-term breakthrough deal unlikely versus 'more of the same.'
insights.speaker: William Roebuck
insights.so_what: Prolonged limbo, not resolution, is the realistic path; trades premised on imminent peace are mispriced.
insights.quote: THE REAL PROBLEM IS DOMESTICALLY. IT'S A REAL NEEDLE HE HAS TO THREAD.

insights.claim: Brent crude near $95 and below $100 reflects a contained conflict; the market's true focus is whether it spirals into a wider war or targets oil infrastructure as it did in March, which it currently is not.
insights.speaker: guest, Bloomberg Asia energy reporter
insights.so_what: Spread between current ~$95 Brent and March-war highs is the option premium on conflict spillover; infrastructure attacks are the trigger to watch.

insights.claim: Strait of Hormuz traffic has collapsed to roughly 2 million barrels/day (about one-tenth of pre-war levels) with only ~22 vessels transiting versus 135+ pre-war, and increasing numbers of ships are going dark by turning off AIS transponders and lights.
insights.speaker: Steven (Bloomberg Asia energy reporter)
insights.so_what: Even with a ceasefire, physical oil flows remain structurally tight; AIS-dark tankers mean official flow data understates and overstates risk simultaneously, supporting a persistently tighter market.

insights.claim: Iran's attack on Qatar's key LNG plant in March is expected to cost Qatar roughly $20 billion in lost revenue and may hamper the Qatar Investment Authority's dealmaking push.
insights.speaker: host (Abeer Abu Omar)
insights.so_what: Gulf SWF capital deployment (including into deals like SpaceX) could be constrained by energy-infrastructure revenue hits from the conflict.

themes.theme: US Inflation, Fed Transition, and the Core-vs-Headline Split

insights.claim: May US headline CPI hit a 3-year high driven by Iran-war energy prices, but core was benign and softer than the prior month, providing a counterweight to a hot nonfarm payrolls print ahead of the Fed meeting.
insights.speaker: Rajeev Sibal, Senior Global Economist, Morgan Stanley
insights.so_what: Markets are pricing the energy-driven inflation as transitory; the trade hinges on whether it passes through to core, which Morgan Stanley does not expect within a year.

insights.claim: Morgan Stanley expects the Fed on hold, with the next meeting showing fewer than the prior three dissents, a more neutral statement, and removal of any easing bias.
insights.speaker: Rajeev Sibal
insights.so_what: Position for a hawkish-leaning hold and the removal of easing language, not a hike or cut, at the upcoming meeting.

insights.claim: The upcoming meeting is Kevin Warsh's first as Fed chair, introducing a new communication style the market must learn, adding interpretation risk to every statement and presser.
insights.speaker: Rajeev Sibal
insights.so_what: A regime change in Fed communication is itself a volatility catalyst independent of the policy decision; expect mispricing as markets recalibrate to Warsh.
insights.quote: IT'S GOING TO BE A NEW COMMUNICATION STYLE EVERY TIME WE HAVE A NEW VENTURE TRANSITION, THE MARKET HAS TO LEARN THE STYLE

insights.claim: A clear disinflation signal won't appear until deeper into Q3 (two to three months out) because the market is currently absorbing the top-heavy effects of oil pass-through.
insights.speaker: Rajeev Sibal
insights.so_what: Expect the Fed to stay patient and data-dependent through Q3; rate-cut bets are premature until the oil pass-through clears.

insights.claim: Higher oil is more a growth risk than an inflation risk in Morgan Stanley's framework; only oil above roughly $130-$150 from conflict escalation would constitute a genuine growth scare, alongside a potential pullback in AI capex.
insights.speaker: Rajeev Sibal
insights.so_what: The two identified growth-scare triggers are oil >$130 and an AI-capex pullback; absent either, resilient growth supports equities.

insights.claim: In an oil-premium scenario the key risk is persistence of disrupted supply chains feeding into core inflation (a milder, non-COVID-like version), making persistence versus level over the time horizon the variable Morgan Stanley watches.
insights.speaker: Rajeev Sibal
insights.so_what: Duration of elevated oil matters more than the spot level for the inflation thesis; a multi-month conflict would force a reassessment.

themes.theme: SpaceX IPO: Narrative Premium vs Fundamentals

insights.claim: SpaceX's $75 billion IPO is more than four times oversubscribed, with single orders worth $10 billion or more and Gulf sovereign wealth funds (Saudi PIF, Kuwait Investment Authority, Qatar) each placing $1-5 billion bids.
insights.speaker: guest, Bloomberg Asia equities deputy managing editor
insights.so_what: Extreme oversubscription and SWF anchor demand point to a strong debut pop but heavy allocation rationing; flip-driven retail demand signals froth.

insights.claim: SpaceX is valued at $1.75 trillion on roughly $19 billion of revenue (about 95x revenue), a multiple justified not by current numbers but by the optionality of space narratives like data centers in space, asteroid mining, and Mars colonies.
insights.speaker: guest citing Jim Chanos's critique
insights.so_what: The valuation prices unpriceable hopes/dreams; fundamental investors examining the actual books may not be convinced, setting up a post-hype re-rating risk.
insights.quote: WHEN YOU SELL SPACE, YOU CAN SELL HOPES AND DREAMS

insights.claim: Demand for SpaceX is driven by belief in Elon Musk personally and perceived synergies across Tesla, xAI, and SpaceX, but the intertwined structure creates opacity that slows some institutional investors' enthusiasm.
insights.speaker: guest, Bloomberg Asia equities deputy managing editor
insights.so_what: The Musk-empire entanglement is both the bull case (synergies) and the bear case (opaque structure); watch where valuations settle after initial hype fades.

insights.claim: Newly public mega-IPOs like SpaceX are currently a 'gambling trade' rather than an investment, and need one to four quarters of earnings to determine whether they get priced as secular-growth or cyclical companies.
insights.speaker: Jeffrey Sexton, CEO of Demeter Tactical Investment
insights.so_what: The secular-vs-cyclical re-rating only resolves after several earnings prints; near-term flows are speculative flip demand, not fundamental conviction.
insights.quote: IT IS A GAMBLING TRADE AT THIS POINT, IT IS NOT AN INVESTMENT, BUT IT IS FUN

themes.theme: AI Infrastructure Capex and Frothiness Signals

insights.claim: Oracle shares fell after-hours as Q4 capex came in higher than estimated, overshadowing AI growth, with plans to raise up to $40 billion in equity and debt by FY2027 to fund data-center buildout for OpenAI and other customers.
insights.speaker: host (Abeer Abu Omar)
insights.so_what: The market is punishing capex intensity even amid AI growth; Oracle's $40B funding need signals balance-sheet strain across the AI-infrastructure buildout.

insights.claim: KKR's head of real assets warns markets may be underestimating AI-power events and sees signs of frothiness in digital infrastructure, cautioning that too much may be built relative to current demand.
insights.speaker: host citing KKR head of real assets
insights.so_what: A major real-assets allocator flagging overbuild risk in data centers is an early warning for the AI-infrastructure capex cycle; KKR is positioning for opportunity rather than chasing the growth buzz.

insights.claim: Alibaba fell ~5% on concerns about its AI-token pricing after a Wall Street Journal report that OpenAI is considering massive price cuts.
insights.speaker: guest, Hong Kong markets reporter (Whitney)
insights.so_what: OpenAI price cuts could compress AI-token economics sector-wide; watch margin pressure on Chinese and other AI model providers.

insights.claim: OpenAI says China-linked accounts used ChatGPT to generate English-language posts stirring opposition to US data centers, likely tied to an unnamed private Chinese tech firm.
insights.speaker: host (Abeer Abu Omar)
insights.so_what: An emerging geopolitical-influence vector around the strategic AI-infrastructure industry; low reach now but a flag for foreign attempts to undercut US data-center buildout.

themes.theme: Global Credit, EM Resilience, and ECB Stagflation

insights.claim: Middle East corporates show the lowest default rates of any region and regional growth outshines many others despite the conflict, supported by sustained government economic-diversification spending.
insights.speaker: Victoria Harling, Middle East CIO and co-head of EM corporate debt at Ninety One
insights.so_what: The region investors would expect to suffer most is the most resilient credit; favors Middle East EM corporate debt despite the conflict backdrop.
insights.quote: YOU WOULD EXPECT THE MIDDLE EAST TO BE SUFFERING THE MOST, BUT IT ISN'T

insights.claim: EM sovereigns and corporates that spent recent years extending maturities are better positioned than developed-market peers if rates stay higher for longer, with strong fixed-income inflows even as equity outflows occur.
insights.speaker: Victoria Harling
insights.so_what: Maturity extension plus domestic-flow rebalancing into home markets makes EM credit a higher-for-longer beneficiary; rotate fixed-income exposure toward EM.

insights.claim: AI, not the developed-vs-emerging distinction, is now the real differentiator of capital flows across markets, evident in flows in and out of Korea and Taiwan.
insights.speaker: Victoria Harling
insights.so_what: Reframe cross-market allocation around AI exposure rather than DM/EM labels; AI-levered Asian markets behave as a distinct factor.

insights.claim: The largest six-to-twelve-month risk to EM credit is global growth sentiment, driven by new trade/tariff discussions and USMCA developments, amid a strategic shift toward a multipolar world where EMs increasingly trade among themselves.
insights.speaker: Victoria Harling
insights.so_what: Tariff escalation is the key downside catalyst for EM credit; the multipolar South-South trade push is a structural offset to watch.

insights.claim: The ECB is expected to hike 25bp into a stagflationary dynamic (headline inflation at 3.2%, core accelerating faster than headline), with traders pricing two further hikes this year, though many economists argue this risks repeating the premature-hike error of 2011.
insights.speaker: Oliver Crook, Bloomberg (Frankfurt)
insights.so_what: A hike-into-stagflation that may force aggressive 2011-style cuts later; one cited view sees Q4 hikes reversing, creating a potential policy-whipsaw trade in European rates.

insights.claim: South Africa's plan to fully spin off Eskom's transmission unit (about $2 billion of earnings last year, its most profitable asset) into an independent system operator raises credit concerns from Moody's and bondholders about Eskom's ability to service debt and expand.
insights.speaker: Paul Burkart, Bloomberg Africa energy reporter
insights.so_what: Removing Eskom's most profitable unit weakens its standalone credit; watch Eskom bonds and rating actions as the spinoff proceeds despite Eskom's competitive pushback in court.

insights.claim: The FIFA Men's World Cup, projected to earn FIFA $13 billion, is being overshadowed by US immigration enforcement and first-time dynamic ticket pricing making matches unaffordable, threatening attendance.
insights.speaker: host (Abeer Abu Omar)
insights.so_what: Consumer-facing risk: dynamic pricing plus immigration friction could depress attendance and tourism revenue tied to the tournament.
